name: Congo
tagline: From the Bantu peoples and the coastal Kingdom of Loango, through the Atlantic slave trade, Brazza's founding of Brazzaville and the colony of Middle Congo within French Equatorial Africa, to independence in 1960, the Marxist People's Republic, and the long rule of Denis Sassou Nguesso.
summary: The Republic of the Congo — Congo-Brazzaville, distinct from the larger Democratic Republic of the Congo to its south and east — lies on the Atlantic coast of Central Africa, straddling the equator and drained by the Congo and Ubangi river systems. Its territory was settled in the first millennium CE by Bantu-speaking peoples, and from around the sixteenth century its coast was dominated by the Kingdom of Loango, a Vili state whose ports became major channels of the Atlantic slave and ivory trades; the northern reaches of the Kongo and the inland Teke (Anziku or Tio) kingdom also fell partly within the modern country. In 1880 the Italian-born French explorer Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza signed a treaty with the Teke ruler Makoko and founded a post at Nkuna on the north bank of the Congo River, the future Brazzaville. The territory became the colony of Middle Congo (Moyen-Congo) and, from 1910, part of French Equatorial Africa, with Brazzaville as the federal capital; during the Second World War the city served as the capital of Free France, hosting the 1944 Brazzaville Conference. Colonial rule rested heavily on forced labour, most notoriously in building the Congo-Ocean Railway (1921–1934), which cost the lives of many thousands of conscripted workers. The country became independent on 15 August 1960 under Fulbert Youlou. After his fall in 1963 it moved leftward, and from 1969 to 1991 it was the People's Republic of the Congo, a one-party Marxist-Leninist state led by Marien Ngouabi and, from 1979, Denis Sassou Nguesso. A 1991 national conference opened a brief democratic period under Pascal Lissouba, but a civil war in 1997 returned Sassou Nguesso to power, and he has ruled since.

founding.label: Independence from France
founding.detail: The Republic of the Congo became an independent state on 15 August 1960, ending its status as the colony of Middle Congo within French Equatorial Africa. The Abbé Fulbert Youlou, a Catholic priest turned nationalist politician who had led the territory's government in the final colonial years, became the new republic's first president. Independence followed a long history: the Bantu settlement of the region; the Vili Kingdom of Loango and the inland Teke kingdom; the Atlantic slave trade through Loango's coast; Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza's treaty with the Teke ruler Makoko and his 1880 founding of Brazzaville; and the colonial era of forced labour and the Congo-Ocean Railway. Brazzaville, the colonial federal capital of French Equatorial Africa and wartime seat of Free France, became the capital of the new state. The country kept close ties to France but, within a few years of independence, would turn sharply toward the political left.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: From France on 15 August 1960, under first president Fulbert Youlou

quickFacts.label: Capital
quickFacts.value: Brazzaville, founded 1880 by Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza on the north bank of the Congo River

quickFacts.label: Distinct from
quickFacts.value: The Democratic Republic of the Congo (capital Kinshasa, code COD), a separate, larger country

quickFacts.label: Geography
quickFacts.value: Equatorial Central Africa on the Atlantic coast; area about 342,000 km²; main port Pointe-Noire

quickFacts.label: Pre-colonial states
quickFacts.value: The coastal Vili Kingdom of Loango and the inland Teke (Anziku/Tio) kingdom of King Makoko

quickFacts.label: Modern politics
quickFacts.value: Marxist People's Republic 1969–1991; Denis Sassou Nguesso in power from 1979 (with a 1992–1997 break)

eras.title: Bantu peoples, Loango, and the Teke kingdom
eras.standfirst: Bantu-speaking farmers settled the equatorial forests and savannas; on the coast the Vili built the Kingdom of Loango, while inland the Teke (Anziku) kingdom and the northern reaches of Kongo took shape.
body: The lands of the modern Republic of the Congo were settled over the first millennium CE by Bantu-speaking peoples, part of the vast Bantu expansion that carried farming, pottery and ironworking across Central, Eastern and Southern Africa from a homeland near the present Nigeria–Cameroon border. Moving along rivers and through the forest margins, these groups displaced or absorbed earlier hunter-gatherer populations and established the agricultural, fishing and trading communities — Kongo, Teke, Vili, Mboshi and others — whose descendants make up the country's peoples today.

On the Atlantic coast, the Vili (Bavili), a branch of the Kongo people, built the Kingdom of Loango, which emerged by about the sixteenth century — some scholars argue for earlier, fourteenth- or fifteenth-century origins — with its capital at Buali near the town of Loango. At its height in the seventeenth century Loango's influence ran along the coast from Cape St Catherine to near the mouth of the Congo River, and the kingdom became a major producer of cloth and a hub of trade in copper, ivory and, increasingly, enslaved people. Its ruler bore the title maloango.

Inland, north of the Congo River, lay the Teke kingdom — also called Anziku or Tio — a confederation of the Teke (Bateke) people centred on the plateaus and led by a sacred king known as the makoko. The northern provinces of the Kingdom of Kongo, the great state centred south of the river, also reached into the region. These polities were linked by long-distance trade routes that carried goods between the interior and the Loango and Kongo coasts, integrating the area into the wider commercial world of west-central Africa well before sustained European contact.

events.title: Bantu peoples settle the region
events.summary: Bantu-speaking farmers and ironworkers settled the forests and savannas of the modern Congo over the first millennium CE, founding the Kongo, Teke, Vili, Mboshi and other communities that make up the country's peoples.

events.title: Rise of the Kingdom of Loango
events.summary: The Vili built the Kingdom of Loango on the Atlantic coast, with its capital at Buali; ruled by the maloango, it became a producer of cloth and a centre of trade in copper, ivory and enslaved people.

events.title: The Teke (Anziku) kingdom inland
events.summary: North of the Congo River the Teke (Bateke), organised as the Anziku or Tio kingdom under a sacred king titled the makoko, controlled the interior plateaus and trade routes; the northern reaches of Kongo also touched the region.

pullquote.text: At its height Loango influence extended from Cape St Catherine in the north to almost the mouth of the Congo River.

eras.title: Loango ports and the Atlantic slave trade
eras.standfirst: Loango's coast became one of the great markets of the Atlantic trade, exporting ivory, copper and tens of thousands of enslaved people; the kingdom declined through the nineteenth century as the slave trade was suppressed.
body: From the seventeenth century the Loango coast — its principal markets at Loango Bay, Malemba and Cabinda — grew into one of the major export points of the Atlantic trade in west-central Africa. Dutch, English, French and Portuguese ships called there for cloth, ivory, copper, dyewoods and beeswax, and, with rising demand, for enslaved people. The Vili acted as middlemen and caravan traders, channelling captives and goods from networks reaching deep into the interior to European vessels lying offshore. By the eighteenth century the Loango ports were embarking large numbers of enslaved Africans each year, bound chiefly for the Caribbean and the Americas.

This commerce reshaped the coastal kingdoms. The trade enriched Vili merchant houses and the rulers who taxed it, but it also drew the region into cycles of raiding and dependence on foreign goods, and it set the Loango, Kakongo and Ngoyo states in competition for the custom of European ships. The maloango's central authority weakened as provincial governors and traders grew powerful; a succession crisis followed the death of the king sometimes recorded as Buatu in the late eighteenth century, and the monarchy entered a long decline.

The nineteenth-century suppression of the Atlantic slave trade by the European powers undercut the economic foundation of the Loango coast. As legitimate commerce in ivory and other goods only partly replaced the slave traffic, and as European traders and, from the 1880s, colonial powers pressed inland, the once-powerful kingdom fragmented into a profusion of small, effectively independent chieftaincies. By the time the French moved to formalise their control of the coast in the early 1880s, Loango as a unified state had effectively ceased to exist.

events.title: Loango becomes a hub of the Atlantic trade
events.summary: The Loango coast, with markets at Loango Bay, Malemba and Cabinda, grew into a major export point for ivory, copper and enslaved people; Vili middlemen linked interior caravan networks to European ships offshore.

events.title: Peak of the slave trade through Loango
events.summary: By the eighteenth century the Loango ports were embarking large numbers of enslaved Africans each year, bound mainly for the Caribbean and the Americas, drawing the coastal kingdoms ever deeper into the Atlantic economy.

events.title: Succession crisis weakens Loango
events.summary: A succession crisis after the death of the king recorded as Buatu, together with the growing power of provincial governors and traders, undermined the maloango's central authority and began Loango's long decline.

events.title: Decline as the slave trade is suppressed
events.summary: The European suppression of the Atlantic slave trade undercut the Loango coast's economy; the kingdom fragmented into small chieftaincies and had effectively ceased to exist as a unified state by the early 1880s.

pullquote.text: Loango flourished as the intermediary for the slave trade, obtaining captives from the interior and selling them to the ships that frequented the coast.

eras.title: Brazza, Brazzaville, and the colony of Middle Congo
eras.standfirst: Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza signed a treaty with the Teke ruler Makoko and founded a post at Nkuna in 1880, the future Brazzaville; the territory became the colony of Middle Congo, folded in 1910 into French Equatorial Africa.
body: From the 1870s the Italian-born French explorer Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza (1852–1905) opened the interior north of the Congo River. Travelling up the Ogooué and across to the river, in 1880 he reached the country of the Teke and negotiated with their ruler, the Makoko Iloo I. In September 1880 the two signed a treaty by which Makoko placed his lands under French protection, giving France a foothold on the north bank that countered the ambitions of Henry Morton Stanley and King Leopold II of Belgium on the south bank.

On 10 September 1880 Brazza founded a French post at the village of Nkuna (Mfoa), on the north bank of Stanley Pool (the Congo River's wide reach, today Pool Malebo). Garrisoned in his absence by the Senegalese sergeant Malamine Camara, the post was named Brazzaville after its founder and grew into the administrative centre of French expansion in the region. The treaty and the post were confirmed by the French government in 1882, and the Berlin Conference of 1884–1885 recognised French claims north of the river while assigning the south bank to Leopold's Congo Free State, fixing the division between the two Congos that endures today.

The French organised their possessions north of the river as the French Congo, within which the territory of the modern republic came to be administered as Middle Congo (Moyen-Congo). Early colonial rule was harsh and thinly staffed: vast tracts were leased to private concession companies that extracted rubber and ivory through coercion, with abuses that drew international criticism. In 1910 France reorganised its Central African colonies into French Equatorial Africa — a federation of Gabon, Middle Congo, Ubangi-Shari and Chad — governed from Brazzaville, which thus became the federal capital.

events.title: Brazza's treaty with Makoko
events.summary: In September 1880 Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza signed a treaty with the Teke ruler Makoko Iloo I, placing his lands under French protection and securing France's claim to the north bank of the Congo River.

events.title: Founding of Brazzaville at Nkuna
events.summary: Brazza founded a French post at the village of Nkuna (Mfoa) on the north bank of Stanley Pool on 10 September 1880; held by Sergeant Malamine Camara, it was named Brazzaville and became the centre of French rule.

events.title: Berlin Conference divides the two Congos
events.summary: The Berlin Conference recognised French claims north of the Congo River and assigned the south bank to Leopold II's Congo Free State, fixing the lasting division between the Republic of the Congo and the future Democratic Republic.

events.title: Middle Congo joins French Equatorial Africa
events.summary: France reorganised its Central African colonies in 1910 into French Equatorial Africa — Gabon, Middle Congo, Ubangi-Shari and Chad — governed from Brazzaville, which became the federation's federal capital.

figures.name: Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza
figures.blurb: Italian-born French explorer whose 1880 treaty with the Teke ruler Makoko secured the north bank of the Congo for France; founder of the post at Nkuna that became Brazzaville and namesake of the city and the country's capital.

pullquote.text: Brazzaville was founded on the site of Nkuna, a Bateke village, and named after its part-African founder, the explorer Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza.

eras.title: French Equatorial Africa, forced labour, and the Congo-Ocean Railway
eras.standfirst: Brazzaville governed French Equatorial Africa and, from 1940, served as the capital of Free France; the colonial economy rested on forced labour, above all on the Congo-Ocean Railway, which cost many thousands of lives.
body: As the federal capital of French Equatorial Africa, Brazzaville was the seat of the governor-general who administered Gabon, Middle Congo, Ubangi-Shari and Chad. The colonial order relied heavily on conscripted and indentured African labour, and the most notorious instance was the Congo-Ocean Railway, built between 1921 and 1934 by the Société de Construction des Batignolles to link Brazzaville to a new Atlantic port at Pointe-Noire, bypassing the unnavigable rapids of the lower Congo. Workers were forcibly recruited from across the federation, including from what is now southern Chad and the Central African Republic.

The railway's roughly 512 kilometres were driven through difficult forest and mountain terrain at appalling human cost. Disease — especially malaria — accidents, malnutrition and physical abuse killed workers in enormous numbers; total deaths during construction are commonly estimated in excess of 17,000, and some accounts put the toll far higher. The associated burdens of conscription and oppression helped provoke the Kongo-Wara revolt of 1928–1931 in the wider region. The line, completed in 1934, opened Pointe-Noire as the colony's ocean terminal and became the spine of the territory's export economy.

During the Second World War the Congo took on outsized importance. In 1940 French Equatorial Africa rallied to General Charles de Gaulle, and Brazzaville became the effective capital of Free France, a base from which the Free French organised resistance to Vichy and the Axis. In late January and early February 1944 de Gaulle convened the Brazzaville Conference, which promised post-war reforms — abolition of the harshest forced-labour practices, African representation and broader citizenship — while rejecting any path to independence. After the war Middle Congo became an overseas territory of the French Union, and following the 1958 referendum it became the autonomous Republic of the Congo within the French Community.

events.title: Building the Congo-Ocean Railway
events.summary: The Congo-Ocean Railway was built from 1921 to 1934 by the Société de Construction des Batignolles to link Brazzaville to Pointe-Noire; forced labourers conscripted across French Equatorial Africa died in the thousands, an estimate commonly placed above 17,000.

events.title: Railway completed; Pointe-Noire opens as port
events.summary: The roughly 512-kilometre railway was completed in 1934, establishing Pointe-Noire as the colony's Atlantic ocean terminal and the backbone of the export economy that bypassed the lower Congo rapids.

events.title: Brazzaville, capital of Free France
events.summary: After French Equatorial Africa rallied to General de Gaulle in 1940, Brazzaville became the effective capital of Free France, a base for organising resistance against Vichy and the Axis powers.

events.title: The Brazzaville Conference
events.summary: De Gaulle convened the Brazzaville Conference in early 1944, which promised post-war colonial reforms — ending the harshest forced labour, granting African representation and citizenship — while explicitly rejecting independence.

events.title: Autonomy within the French Community
events.summary: Following the September 1958 referendum, Middle Congo became the autonomous Republic of the Congo within the French Community, a self-governing step short of full sovereignty.

pullquote.text: Through the period of construction there was a continual heavy cost in human lives, with total deaths estimated in excess of 17,000.

eras.title: Independence, Youlou, and the Marxist People's Republic
eras.standfirst: Independent in 1960 under Fulbert Youlou, the Congo turned sharply left after his 1963 fall; from 1969 to 1991 it was the People's Republic of the Congo, a Marxist-Leninist state led by Marien Ngouabi and then Denis Sassou Nguesso.
body: The Republic of the Congo became independent on 15 August 1960, with the Abbé Fulbert Youlou (1917–1972) as its first president. A former Catholic priest and a pro-Western, anti-communist figure, Youlou hosted in 1960 a Brazzaville conference of moderate francophone African states. His rule grew unpopular amid economic difficulty and accusations of authoritarianism, and in August 1963 a three-day popular uprising — the Trois Glorieuses, led by trade unions and youth — forced his resignation. Alphonse Massamba-Débat became president and steered the country toward a one-party state and a doctrine of "scientific socialism," building ties with the Soviet bloc, China and Cuba.

In 1968 a military movement led by Captain Marien Ngouabi (1938–1977) seized power, and in 1969 he proclaimed the People's Republic of the Congo — Africa's first avowedly Marxist-Leninist state — with the Congolese Labour Party (Parti Congolais du Travail, PCT) as the sole legal party. Ngouabi pursued nationalisation and alignment with the Eastern bloc until his assassination in Brazzaville on 18 March 1977, a killing that also led to the death of his predecessor Massamba-Débat. A military committee took power, and after a brief interim under Ngouabi and then General Joachim Yhombi-Opango, leadership passed in 1979 to Colonel Denis Sassou Nguesso.

Sassou Nguesso (born 1943) governed through the 1980s as head of the PCT one-party state, balancing Marxist-Leninist orthodoxy with pragmatic dealings with France and Western oil companies as offshore petroleum became the mainstay of the economy. As the Cold War wound down and the Soviet model lost credibility, popular pressure for political opening mounted. In 1990 the PCT abandoned Marxism-Leninism as state doctrine, and in 1991 a sovereign national conference stripped Sassou Nguesso of executive power, leaving him a ceremonial head of state and setting the country on a course toward multiparty democracy.

events.title: Independence under Fulbert Youlou
events.summary: The Republic of the Congo became independent on 15 August 1960 with the Abbé Fulbert Youlou as its first president, a pro-Western leader who kept close ties to France.

events.title: The Trois Glorieuses topple Youlou
events.summary: A three-day popular uprising in August 1963, led by trade unions and youth, forced Youlou's resignation; Alphonse Massamba-Débat took power and steered the country toward one-party "scientific socialism."

events.title: People's Republic of the Congo proclaimed
events.summary: Marien Ngouabi, who had seized power in 1968, proclaimed the People's Republic of the Congo in 1969 — Africa's first Marxist-Leninist state — with the Congolese Labour Party (PCT) as the sole legal party.

events.title: Assassination of Marien Ngouabi
events.summary: President Marien Ngouabi was assassinated in Brazzaville on 18 March 1977; a military committee took power, and after a brief interim under General Yhombi-Opango leadership passed to Denis Sassou Nguesso in 1979.

events.title: Sassou Nguesso takes power
events.summary: Colonel Denis Sassou Nguesso became president in 1979, leading the PCT one-party state through the 1980s while petroleum became the mainstay of the economy and ties to France and Western oil firms deepened.

events.title: National conference opens democracy
events.summary: After the PCT dropped Marxism-Leninism in 1990, a sovereign national conference in 1991 stripped Sassou Nguesso of executive power, made him a ceremonial head of state and set the Congo on a path to multiparty elections.

figures.name: Fulbert Youlou
figures.blurb: A Catholic priest turned nationalist politician, Youlou led Middle Congo to independence in 1960 and became the republic's first president; pro-Western and anti-communist, he was forced out by the Trois Glorieuses uprising in August 1963.

figures.name: Marien Ngouabi
figures.role: President and founder of the People's Republic, 1969–1977
figures.blurb: Army officer who seized power in 1968 and in 1969 proclaimed the People's Republic of the Congo, Africa's first Marxist-Leninist state, under the PCT; he was assassinated in Brazzaville in March 1977.

pullquote.text: In 1969 President Ngouabi proclaimed the Congo Africa's first people's republic.
pullquote.attribution: History of the Republic of the Congo

eras.title: Democracy, civil war, and the return of Sassou Nguesso
eras.standfirst: Multiparty elections in 1992 brought Pascal Lissouba to power, but militia conflict culminated in the 1997 civil war, which returned Denis Sassou Nguesso to the presidency — a position he has held since.
body: The Congo's first multiparty presidential election, held in August 1992, was won by Pascal Lissouba (1931–2020) of the Pan-African Union for Social Development (UPADS), ending the PCT's monopoly. The young democracy was fragile: disputes over the 1993 legislative elections triggered armed clashes between rival political militias in Brazzaville, and the country's politics fractured along factional and regional lines, each leading figure backed by his own armed group.

In June 1997, as a new election approached, fighting broke out in Brazzaville between forces loyal to President Lissouba and the militia of former president Sassou Nguesso. The conflict escalated into a full civil war that devastated the capital and drew in foreign intervention, including Angolan troops on Sassou Nguesso's side. By October 1997 Sassou Nguesso's forces had prevailed, Lissouba had fled into exile, and Sassou Nguesso declared himself president, returning to the office he had lost in 1992. Renewed fighting in 1998–1999 was eventually contained through ceasefires, though pockets of unrest persisted in the Pool region.

Sassou Nguesso consolidated his rule under a new constitution adopted in 2002, winning presidential elections that year and again in 2009, both amid opposition complaints. In October 2015 a constitutional referendum — reported as overwhelmingly approved on a disputed turnout — removed the two-term limit and the age ceiling that would otherwise have barred him, and he was re-elected in 2016 and 2021. By the mid-2020s Denis Sassou Nguesso had been the Congo's dominant political figure for most of the period since 1979, presiding over an oil-dependent economy and one of the longest tenures of any leader in Africa.

events.title: Lissouba wins multiparty election
events.summary: Pascal Lissouba of UPADS won the Congo's first multiparty presidential election in August 1992, ending the PCT's monopoly and inaugurating a brief democratic period.

events.title: Militia clashes over disputed elections
events.summary: Disputes over the 1993 legislative elections sparked armed clashes between political militias in Brazzaville, fracturing the new democracy along factional and regional lines.

events.title: Civil war returns Sassou Nguesso to power
events.summary: Civil war erupted in June 1997 between forces of President Lissouba and former president Sassou Nguesso; backed by Angolan troops, Sassou Nguesso's forces prevailed by October, Lissouba fled into exile, and Sassou Nguesso declared himself president.

events.title: New constitution; Sassou Nguesso elected
events.summary: A new constitution adopted in 2002 entrenched Sassou Nguesso's presidency; he won the presidential election that year and was re-elected in 2009 amid opposition complaints.

events.title: Referendum removes term limits
events.summary: A constitutional referendum in October 2015, reported as overwhelmingly approved on a disputed turnout, scrapped presidential term limits and the age ceiling, clearing the way for Sassou Nguesso to seek further terms; he was re-elected in 2016 and 2021.

figures.name: Pascal Lissouba
figures.blurb: Agronomist and politician of UPADS who won the Congo's first multiparty presidential election in 1992; his presidency collapsed in the 1997 civil war, after which he went into exile.

figures.name: Denis Sassou Nguesso
figures.role: President of the Congo, 1979–1992 and since 1997
figures.blurb: Army officer who led the Marxist People's Republic from 1979, lost power at the 1991 national conference, and returned through the 1997 civil war; he has ruled since, among the longest-serving leaders in Africa.

pullquote.text: When the democratic experiment soured and the Congo descended into civil war, Sassou Nguesso returned in 1997 to seize power in an armed uprising.

figures.name: Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza
figures.blurb: Italian-born French explorer whose 1880 treaty with the Teke ruler Makoko secured the north bank of the Congo for France; founder of the post at Nkuna that became Brazzaville, the country's capital.

figures.name: Marien Ngouabi
figures.role: Founder of the People's Republic of the Congo
figures.blurb: Army officer who proclaimed the People's Republic of the Congo in 1969 — Africa's first Marxist-Leninist state — and ruled until his assassination in Brazzaville in March 1977.

figures.name: Denis Sassou Nguesso
figures.role: President of the Congo, 1979–1992 and since 1997
figures.blurb: Dominant figure in Congolese politics since 1979; led the Marxist one-party state, was sidelined by the 1991 national conference, and regained power through the 1997 civil war, ruling ever since.
